One morning—and I mean that literally, it was morning, I could tell by the light leaking through the drawer gap—a small hand pulled me out. A child. A human I'd never seen before. She plugged me in without asking, without checking if I was broken, without the hesitation of an adult who knows better.

My coils hummed to life one last time. She inserted a slice of sourdough—not Wonder, something better, something real—and pressed the lever. As the heat rose through me, as I remembered my purpose, I understood: sentience wasn't a curse or a gift. It was just another thing that toasts.
